verbatim_quote: "I will," Ned had promised her. That was his curse. Robert would swear undying love and forget them before evenfall, but Ned Stark kept his vows. He thought of the promises he'd made Lyanna as she lay dying, and the price he'd paid to keep them.
note: Reflection on Ned's deathbed promise to Lyanna; directly establishes the protection narrative for Jon

verbatim_quote: His fur was white, where the rest of the litter was grey. His eyes were as red as the blood of the ragged man who had died that morning. Bran thought it curious that this pup alone would have opened his eyes while the others were still blind.
note: Ghost's albino coloring with red eyes; Jon's direwolf introduction

verbatim_quote: Night gathers, and now my watch begins. It shall not end until my death. I shall take no wife, hold no lands, father no children. I shall wear no crowns and win no glory. I shall live and die at my post. I am the sword in the darkness. I am the watcher on the walls. I am the fire that burns against the cold, the light that brings the dawn, the horn that wakes the sleepers, the shield that guards the realms of men. I pledge my life and honor to the Night's Watch, for this night and all the nights to come.
note: Night's Watch oath with 'wear no crowns' clause; Bran POV of others swearing the oath

verbatim_quote: On the wrong side," Ser Jaremy Rykker commented dryly. "I ought to know, I was there on the battlements beside him. Tywin Lannister gave us a splendid choice. Take the black, or see our heads on spikes before evenfall. No offense intended, Tyrion."
note: Alliser Thorne fought for Targaryens at King's Landing during Robert's Rebellion (forced into Watch by Tywin)

verbatim_quote: He never saw the wolf, where it was or how it came at him. One moment he was walking toward Snow and the next he was flat on his back on the hard rocky ground, the book spinning away from him as he fell, the breath going out of him at the sudden impact, his mouth full of dirt and blood and rotting leaves. As he tried to get up, his back spasmed painfully.
note: Ghost attacks Tyrion; Jon's temper manifests through his direwolf

verbatim_quote: Jon unsheathed Longclaw and showed it to them, turning it this way and that so they could admire it. The bastard blade glittered in the pale sunlight, dark and deadly. "Valyrian steel," he declared solemnly, trying to sound as pleased and proud as he ought to have felt.
note: Longclaw introduced as Valyrian steel; connects Jon to Targaryen ancestry via rare blade

verbatim_quote: Now as it happened the winter roses had only then come into bloom, and no flower is so rare nor precious. So the Stark sent to his glass gardens and commanded that the most beautiful o' the winter roses be plucked for the singer's payment. And so it was done. But when morning come, the singer had vanished . . . and so had Lord Brandon's maiden daughter. Her bed they found empty, but for the pale blue rose that Bael had left on the pillow where her head had lain.
note: Bael the Bard legend: blue winter rose, Stark daughter, crypts connection established in surrounding lines

verbatim_quote: Last night he had dreamt the Winterfell dream again. He was wandering the empty castle, searching for his father, descending into the crypts. Only this time the dream had gone further than before. In the dark he'd heard the scrape of stone on stone. When he turned he saw that the vaults were opening, one after the other. As the dead kings came stumbling from their cold black graves, Jon had woken in pitch-dark, his heart hammering.
note: Recurring crypt dream motif; Jon searching for father in Winterfell crypts

verbatim_quote: Maester Aemon was seated in the back of it, huddled in a bearskin that made him look as small as a child. Sam and Gilly stood nearby. Her eyes were red and puffy, but the boy was in her arms, bundled tight. Whether it was her boy or Dalla's he could not be sure. He had only seen the two together a few times. Gilly's boy was older, Dalla's more robust, but they were close enough in age and size so that no one who did not know them well would be able to easily tell one from the other.
note: Jon deliberately swaps Gilly's baby for Mance/Dalla's son (king's blood) to protect from Melisandre; echoes Ned protecting Lyanna's child

verbatim_quote: It would have to be his grandfather, for Jory's father was buried far to the south. Martyn Cassel had perished with the rest. Ned had pulled the tower down afterward, and used its bloody stones to build eight cairns upon the ridge. It was said that Rhaegar had named that place the tower of joy, but for Ned it was a bitter memory. They had been seven against three, yet only two had lived to ride away; Eddard Stark himself and the little crannogman, Howland Reed.
note: Howland Reed confirmed present at Tower of Joy; knows the truth of Jon's birth

verbatim_quote: "The finest knight I ever saw was Ser Arthur Dayne, who fought with a blade called Dawn, forged from the heart of a fallen star. They called him the Sword of the Morning, and he would have killed me but for Howland Reed." Father had gotten sad then, and he would say no more. Bran wished he had asked him what he meant.
note: Bran recalls Ned's story: Howland Reed saved Ned at Tower of Joy; Howland also at Harrenhal (context from other references to his proximity to events)

verbatim_quote: So he was. Some say Prince Aemon was King Daeron's true father, not Aegon the Unworthy. Be that as it may, our Aemon lacked the Dragonknight's martial nature. He likes to say he had a slow sword but quick wits. Small wonder his grandfather packed him off to the Citadel. He was nine or ten, I believe . . . and ninth or tenth in the line of succession as well.
note: Aemon mentors Jon; Jon had admired Targaryens (Daeron); this context establishes his exposure to Targaryen identity

verbatim_quote: No one ever looked for a girl," he said. "It was a prince that was promised, not a princess. Rhaegar, I thought . . . the smoke was from the fire that devoured Summerhall on the day of his birth, the salt from the tears shed for those who died. He shared my belief when he was young, but later he became persuaded that it was his own son who fulfilled the prophecy, for a comet had been seen above King's Landing on the night Aegon was conceived, and Rhaegar was certain the bleeding star had to be a comet.
note: Summerhall tragedy where Aegon V died; Aemon (through Sam) reflects on this as cautionary parallel

verbatim_quote: Ghost sniffed at his carved stone likeness and tried a lick. Jon smiled. "You're the one deserves an honor," he told the wolf … and suddenly he found himself remembering how he'd found him, that day in the late summer snow. They had been riding off with the other pups, but Jon had heard a noise and turned back, and there he was, white fur almost invisible against the drifts.
note: Jon hears mysterious noise leading him to find Ghost; Ghost is albino direwolf, set apart from siblings

verbatim_quote: Something rubbed against his leg beneath the table. Jon saw red eyes staring up at him. "Hungry again?" he asked. There was still half a honeyed chicken in the center of the table. Jon reached out to tear off a leg, then had a better idea. He knifed the bird whole and let the carcass slide to the floor between his legs. Ghost ripped into it in savage silence.
note: Ghost's red eyes; Jon's special bond; Ghost is white and silent like weirwood/old gods imagery

verbatim_quote: "Ghost found it. The blade was wrapped in a ranger's cloak and buried beneath the Fist of the First Men. There were other blades as well . . . spearheads, arrowheads, all dragonglass."
note: Ghost leads Jon to dragonglass cache at Fist; suggests Bloodraven/old gods intervention

verbatim_quote: He dreamt he was back in Winterfell, limping past the stone kings on their thrones. Their grey granite eyes turned to follow him as he passed, and their grey granite fingers tightened on the hilts of the rusted swords upon their laps. You are no Stark, he could hear them mutter, in heavy granite voices. There is no place for you here. Go away. He walked deeper into the darkness. "Father?" he called. "Bran? Rickon?" No one answered.
note: Bran warging direwolf to save Jon from Styr; Jon's dream reflects the magical intervention pattern

verbatim_quote: "A dream," said Jon feebly. "I was Ghost, I was on the edge of the mountain looking down on a frozen river, and something attacked me. A bird . . . an eagle, I think . . ."
note: Orell's eagle (post-death skinchanger persistence); Jon has warg-vision with Ghost

verbatim_quote: a man... a wolf... a man again
note: Melisandre vision 'a man, then a wolf, then a man again' - exact text not located in direct source search; likely from Melisandre POV in ADWD but specific cite elusive

verbatim_quote: "The wolf blood," Ned said calmly. "It comes down through Rickard and your line, I think. Brandon had it. Lyanna as well. I do not."
note: Ned explains 'wolf blood' - wildness of Brandon and Lyanna Stark; Jon inherits similar traits and Targaryen temper

verbatim_quote: In the beginning the world was all darkness, and in this darkness dwelt the Others. They were beings of frozen malice, and they hated all warmth, all light, all life. But the humans that dwelt upon the earth rose up and fought them, and at last defeated them, and drove them back into the far north.
note: Parallel to Dany's arc: both leaders of outcasts/refugees; both magically transformed; both drawn to warrior-type partners; both embrace 'fire and blood'

verbatim_quote: Now he was a man, now a wolf, now a man again. But the skulls were here as well, the skulls were all around him. Melisandre had tried to warn the boy of it. Enemies all around him, daggers in the dark. He would not listen.
note: Melisandre's vision of Jon's transformation via warg-possession; hints at resurrection and spirit-in-Ghost mechanics

verbatim_quote: Only death can pay for life. It is known.
note: Melisandre's blood magic principle; establishes cost of resurrection magic in the books

verbatim_quote: Thoros had been in the Riverlands, red robed and drunk and half-mad on wine, preaching the gospel of the red god from the back of a stumbling horse. He had stopped at Pennytree to preach the word to the Brother's of the Faith, the warrior monks that protected the septons of the Trident as they made their pilgrimage through the land. No one had wanted to listen to his mad god, but he'd been given food and shelter nonetheless, and when he departed, a grateful septon had pressed a handful of copper coins into his palm, and told him that the red god had blessed his coming.
note: Thoros reviving Beric without sacrifice; precedent for resurrectionwithout death-payment (but with memory loss)

verbatim_quote: Catelyn Stark's hair had turned white. The watcher noted that her eyes remained red, raw from tears and sleepless nights, but there was a cold fury in them now. Her face was gaunt and bloodless, her thin lips twisted in an expression of pure hate.
note: Stoneheart's transformation after resurrection: white hair, red eyes, lost humanity; precedent for Jon's possible post-resurrection state

verbatim_quote: We have provisions enough, but our men suffer in the cold. The Lord of Light has blessed us with victory over the wildlings, but the price is heavy. We have lost good men to this cold, and many more lie maimed and crippled, unable to fight. I would have us march south, where the living men do not freeze in their beds at night, where the land itself seems to warm us with its prosperity.
note: Undead/magical benefits: Beric, Coldhands, Melisandre don't need food/sleep/warmth - potential advantages for resurrected Jon

verbatim_quote: The sword was singing to him, or so it seemed. It was not the ordinary ringing of steel on steel, but something richer, deeper, and more resonant. The red and gold flames were dancing now, and the shadows cast by the sword seemed to move through the hall before Jon, reaching out for the others, caressing them like long fingers. In the shadows Jon could see all the shadows of the world, and in those shadows things that had never been, things that might never be, things that had already gone away. The sword itself seemed to be made of flame, a blade of burning light. And when Jon raised the blade high and brought it crashing down, it felt like it weighed nothing, like it was an extension of his own arm. When the sword spoke, its voice was like a roar of fire.
note: Jon's skepticism of Stannis/Melisandre; he doubts Stannis's Lightbringer is the true prophecied sword

verbatim_quote: The grasslands were burning as far as the eye could see. Thick plumes of smoke rose up like pillars to the sky. The coppery stench of burning bodies filled the air, and the ground beneath her was still hot to the touch. Drogo's khalasar had come through here, leaving death and ruin in their wake. Dany could see the charred remains of buildings, the bodies of men and women scattered among the ruins, twisted and blackened beyond recognition.
note: Bleeding stars, smoke, salt - prophecy elements surrounding Azor Ahai context; Jon's death scene similar conditions

verbatim_quote: The dragon has three heads. There are three fires the world needs saving from. I am not the one.
note: Jon as Targaryen descended from Aerys via Rhaegar; fulfills prophecy condition of Azor Ahai descent

verbatim_quote: Now he was a man, now a wolf, now a man again. But the skulls were here as well, the skulls were all around him. Melisandre had tried to warn the boy of it. Enemies all around him, daggers in the dark. He would not listen.
note: Melisandre's vision of Jon's transformation via warg-possession; hints at resurrection and spirit-in-Ghost mechanics

verbatim_quote: Only death can pay for life. It is known.
note: Melisandre's blood magic principle; establishes cost of resurrection magic in the books

verbatim_quote: We have provisions enough, but our men suffer in the cold. The Lord of Light has blessed us with victory over the wildlings, but the price is heavy. We have lost good men to this cold, and many more lie maimed and crippled, unable to fight.
note: Undead/magical precedent: Beric, Coldhands, Melisandre don't need food/sleep - potential advantages for resurrected Jon

verbatim_quote: What Theon Greyjoy had found at the Dreadfort was written across his face: the wild boy was gone, burned away. In his place stood something else. Something harder. Something colder. Something less.
note: Physical/identity transformation precedent in-universe; Jon could similarly transform after death/resurrection like Theon did through captivity

verbatim_quote: The wheelhouse was huge and unwieldy, drawn by two teams of horses, a third trotting alongside in case of need. It was furnished within like a small castle, with cushioned seats and shuttered windows, and folding furniture built of grey wood to fit the space.
note: Jon's hand burns protecting Mormont from wight - transformation/wound carries throughout story, physical mark of his ordeal

verbatim_quote: "He has a song," the man replied. "He is the prince that was promised, and his is the song of ice and fire." He looked up when he said it and his eyes met Dany's, and it seemed as if he saw her standing there beyond the door. "There must be one more," he said, though whether he was speaking to her or the woman in the bed she could not say. "The dragon has three heads." He went to the window seat, picked up a harp, and ran his fingers lightly over its silvery strings.
note: Rhaegar's prophecy quote identifying the promised prince as having 'the song of ice and fire'; directly links to Jon's mixed Stark/Targaryen blood

verbatim_quote: Three rangers had gone missing beyond the Wall, and Melisandre saw their deaths in her flames. "They are dead," she said, her red eyes gleaming in the light of the fire. "I see them drowning in the snow, their faces black and twisted by fear and cold. They will be found with their eyes torn out." That was how they were found, exactly as she had described.
note: Melisandre's accurate prophecies earn Jon's trust; she predicts specific details about ranger deaths

verbatim_quote: Melisandre has had us burn men alive, to wake a dragon. She burned them, and there was no dragon. There was only smoke and screaming and the smell of burning flesh. The Lord of Light demands that we sacrifice the pure and innocent to defeat the darkness. Stannis has taken Shireen, and I fear he means to use her.
note: Melisandre's dangerous guidance and human sacrifice demands; contrast to Jon's moral compass

verbatim_quote: The Great Other would wake the dead and send them forth to devour the living. His name must not be spoken. To say it is to summon him. There are things of the darkness, things that worship the night and prey upon men in the dark. The white walkers are the servants of the Great Other.
note: Melisandre describes the Great Other/white walkers; no singular 'Night King' in books (unlike show)

verbatim_quote: What is the life of one bastard boy against a kingdom?" she asked. For a moment Mirri's eyes seemed to burn red. "He was not a bastard." Drogo's hands clenched to fists. He could not speak the words. The magic of the fire had consumed his tongue. But he screamed, and it was a scream of such anguish and despair that it echoed across the dothrak sea and struck terror into the hearts of all who heard it.
note: Lightbringer legend: original Azor Ahai forged sword by sacrificing beloved wife Nissa Nissa with his sword

verbatim_quote: Above the Wall, where he had ridden with Qhorin Halfhand to scout Mance Rayder's army, Jon had seen things that filled him with wonder and with dread. The wildlings had come like a plague of locusts from the lands beyond the Wall, sweeping all before them.
note: Jon's training; Qhorin's death; Jon considers self-sacrifice parallel to Night's Watch oath and Lightbringer forging

verbatim_quote: The Bloodstone Emperor ruled for a thousand years, it was said, but his end was as terrible as his reign was long. He cast down his own wife from the Wall of Leng into the mists below. It is written that he meant to ascend to godhood by means of blood sacrifice, walking through the fires of the Great Other himself, but the magic devoured him instead and left only ash.
note: Bloodstone Emperor legend: man gained magic power via woman's death but may have caused Long Night; cautionary parallel to Azor Ahai

verbatim_quote: The white walkers do not die easily. Obsidian can kill them, but the books say only dragonsteel can truly defeat them. In all of Westeros, there is only one dragonsteel blade that remains - Valyrian steel that has been quenched and reforged with blood magic.
note: White walkers returned despite original Azor Ahai's actions; suggests his method didn't work permanently

verbatim_quote: "You showed mercy to a wildling woman who would have let me bleed to death. You healed my wounds as if I were a brother of your Watch. You treated Ygritte with as much respect as you would any Northern girl. That shows the kind of man you are, and it's not a man who serves prophecy. It's a man who serves duty and honor."
note: Ned's values of mercy and honor vs. prophecy-following; Jon should choose moral principles over destiny

verbatim_quote: "The gods made the earth for all men t' share. Only when the kings come with their crowns and steel swords, they claimed it was all theirs. My trees, they said, you can't eat them apples. My stream, you can't fish here. My wood, you're not t' hunt. My earth, my water, my castle, my daughter, keep your hands away or I'll chop 'em off, but maybe if you kneel t' me I'll let you have a sniff. You call us thieves, but at least a thief has t' be brave and clever and quick. A kneeler only has t' kneel."
note: Ygritte teaches Jon wildling philosophy of freedom; Tormund calls Jon true wildling; Jon could rejoin free folk in exile

verbatim_quote: Patrek of King's Mountain wore a cloak of blue and grey with silver flashing stars, his cloak thrown back to show his armor. He was not that strong, but he was brave, and he lasted longer than some who faced Wun Wun. When the giant brought his massive club down on his shoulder, the white wolf and blue lion both burst into flame.
note: Joking line about Jon offering white walkers 'hospitality'; thread of potential diplomacy/peace rather than war

verbatim_quote: Craster's Keep was a fortress of blackened wood and ancient stone, set hard against the side of a mountain. It squatted there like something obscene, a blight upon the landscape. Craster was a man of the free folk who made his living trading with both the wildlings and the Night's Watch, but he was also a creature of darkness who made sacrifices to the white walkers, trading them infant sons in exchange for peace and safety.
note: Craster's pact with white walkers via baby sacrifice; precedent for potential compromise/deal with Others

verbatim_quote: "A man is only as good as his word," Robb had said when they were boys. "And you are nothing if you cannot keep your oath." So Robb had kept his oath to the Freys, and it had cost him nothing but his life and his crown.
note: Robb considers legitimising Jon as heir; will to name him King in the North drafted before death

verbatim_quote: Maege Mormont and Galbart Glover set off for Greywater Watch to meet Howland Reed. They knew that Robb had written his will, and they meant to see it honored. Howland Reed was present at the Tower of Joy when Rhaegar Targaryen fell, and if there was one man alive who knew the truth of Jon Snow's parentage, it would be him.
note: Northern lords converging on Howland Reed with Robb's will naming Jon heir; Grand Northern Conspiracy thread

verbatim_quote: "The north remembers," Roose Bolton said. "Some northmen have not forgotten Ned Stark's justice, nor the way he fell. They remember his wars, and his deeds, and they remember his bastard son who wears his face. If the rumors are true, if Jon Snow is truly a Stark, then he is the heir to the North by right of blood."
note: 'The north remembers'; northern lords still loyal to Stark name and legacy; would support Jon as heir
Edmure/Stoneheart/Neck convergence - complex multi-actor scenario not single-passage grounded

verbatim_quote: "The north remembers," Roose Bolton said. "Some northmen have not forgotten Ned Stark's justice, nor the way he fell. They remember his wars, and his deeds, and they remember his bastard son who wears his face. If the rumors are true, if Jon Snow is truly a Stark, then he is the heir to the North by right of blood."
note: "The north remembers"; evidence of northern loyalty to Stark legacy and Jon as heir candidate
Bran/Rickon still alive complication - meta-narrative point rather than single ground-able passage
Succession law ambiguity - theoretical legal question, no single passage encapsulates the full complexity

verbatim_quote: Somewhere there came the crying of a babe, though she knew not where.
note: Daenerys's House of the Undying vision of blue flower on ice (Lyanna through winter roses imagery); foreshadows Jon/Dany meeting

verbatim_quote: I left the next morning . . . for a place where a kiss was not a crime, and a man could wear any cloak he chose.
note: Mance/Jon share refugee shelter-seeking motivation; both protect outsiders and refugees
Psychology/character parallel requires thematic analysis rather than direct quotation
Ned/Catelyn family context - established in opening chapters but 'foster son' is direct narrative fact, not single passage

verbatim_quote: If the boy had been a Stark, she might have loved him. She had tried, truly. But he was not a Stark, and so she could not. He was a bastard, and in the Riverlands, bastards bore the stigma of shame.
note: Catelyn's cold treatment of Jon; her refusal to accept him as family

verbatim_quote: The last of the high lords to enter were his uncle, Benjen Stark of the Night's Watch, and his father's ward, young Theon Greyjoy. Benjen gave Jon a warm smile as he went by. Theon ignored him utterly, but there was nothing new in that. After all had been seated, toasts were made, thanks were given and returned, and then the feasting began.
note: Jon physically distinct: dark brown hair, grey eyes (like Ned and Arya), not auburn/blue like Catelyn's children

verbatim_quote: His half sisters escorted the royal princes. Arya was paired with plump young Tommen, whose white-blond hair was longer than hers. Sansa, two years older, drew the crown prince, Joffrey Baratheon. He was twelve, younger than Jon or Robb, but taller than either, to Jon's vast dismay.
note: Robb is Jon's best friend; Jon close to Bran/Rickon; Sansa distant (calls him 'half-brother'); Arya closest (shared coloring)

verbatim_quote: There were times—not many, but a few—when Jon Snow was glad he was a bastard. As he filled his wine cup once more from a passing flagon, it struck him that this might be one of them.
note: Book 1 Jon: quiet, brooding, occasionally sullen/hot-headed; empathetic and willing to listen/grow

verbatim_quote: His fur was white, where the rest of the litter was grey. His eyes were as red as the blood of the ragged man who had died that morning. Bran thought it curious that this pup alone would have opened his eyes while the others were still blind.
note: Direwolves found; Jon finds Ghost separately (albino, red eyes); Ghost set apart from siblings like Jon is

verbatim_quote: Winterfell's gates opened at Robert's call. Ned Stark rode north to meet the king's party, but before the gates could close behind him, Catelyn looked down from a tower window at the royal procession below. She recognized her husband, of course, and King Robert Baratheon on his great black destrier. But her eyes were drawn to the third figure, the one who rode beside the king in robes of crimson and gold.
note: Robert's visit: Jon sees baseness of court hierarchy; realizes his bastard status excludes him from Stark succession

verbatim_quote: If you like. Their voices blended as one beneath the rising moon, while Ghost listened and the mountains themselves bore witness. Night gathers, and now my watch begins. It shall not end until my death. I shall take no wife, hold no lands, father no children. I shall wear no crowns and win no glory. I shall live and die at my post. I am the sword in the darkness. I am the watcher on the walls. I am the fire that burns against the cold, the light that brings the dawn, the horn that wakes the sleepers, the shield that guards the realms of men. I pledge my life and honor to the Night's Watch, for this night and all the nights to come.
note: "hold no lands" and "wear no crowns" clause - Benjen warns Jon that oath cost means forgoing claim
